title: Zero-Downtime Laravel Deployments With GitHub Actions on a Single VPS
altTitle: How to Deploy Laravel Without Taking Your App Offline
excerpt: Set up a zero-downtime deployment pipeline using GitHub Actions, SSH, and symlink-based releases so your Laravel app stays live through every deploy.

seo.title: Zero-Downtime Laravel Deploy With GitHub Actions
seo.description: Learn how to deploy Laravel to a VPS with zero downtime using GitHub Actions, symlinked releases, and atomic directory swaps. Full workflow included.

content.text: Most early-stage SaaS apps deploy like this: SSH in, git pull, run migrations, hope for the best. During those 20 seconds, Nginx is serving a half-updated app - old PHP files, new database schema, or the other way around. Users hit errors. You refresh and it's fine. You tell yourself it's acceptable.

It's not acceptable, and it's easy to fix. This post walks through a zero-downtime deployment strategy using GitHub Actions and a symlink-based release structure - the same approach Deployer and Envoyer use, but with no extra tools.
How Zero-Downtime Deployments Work

The trick is atomic symlinks. Your web server points at /var/www/app/current. That symlink points to one of your releases - timestamped directories like /var/www/app/releases/20250418120000. When you deploy, you build the new release in a separate directory, run all setup steps, then flip the symlink in one atomic operation. Old release was serving users, new release takes over instantly. No gap.
VPS Directory Structure

/var/www/app/
├── current -> releases/20250418120000   # symlink, Nginx points here
├── releases/
│   ├── 20250418110000/                  # previous release (kept for rollback)
│   └── 20250418120000/                  # current live release
└── shared/
    ├── .env                             # shared across all releases
    └── storage/                         # shared uploads and logs
The shared directory holds anything that persists between deployments - your .env file and storage directory. Each release gets symlinks into shared/ rather than its own copies.
The Deployment Script
Create a deploy.sh script in your repo at scripts/deploy.sh:

#!/bin/bash
set -e

APP_DIR="/var/www/app"
RELEASE="$APP_DIR/releases/$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)"
SHARED="$APP_DIR/shared"

echo "==> Creating release directory: $RELEASE"
mkdir -p "$RELEASE"

echo "==> Cloning latest code"
git clone --depth=1 git@github.com:your-org/your-repo.git "$RELEASE"

echo "==> Linking shared files"
rm -rf "$RELEASE/storage"
ln -nfs "$SHARED/storage" "$RELEASE/storage"
ln -nfs "$SHARED/.env" "$RELEASE/.env"

echo "==> Installing dependencies"
cd "$RELEASE" && composer install --no-dev --optimize-autoloader --quiet

echo "==> Running migrations"
php artisan migrate --force

echo "==> Caching config and routes"
php artisan config:cache
php artisan route:cache
php artisan view:cache

echo "==> Switching symlink (atomic)"
ln -nfs "$RELEASE" "$APP_DIR/current"

echo "==> Restarting queue workers"
php artisan queue:restart

echo "==> Cleaning up old releases (keep last 3)"
ls -dt "$APP_DIR/releases/"* | tail -n +4 | xargs rm -rf

echo "==> Deploy complete!"
The GitHub Actions Workflow
Create .github/workflows/deploy.yml in your repo:

name: Deploy to Production

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  deploy:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest

    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Set up SSH
        uses: webfactory/ssh-agent@v0.9.0
        with:
          ssh-private-key: ${{ secrets.DEPLOY_SSH_KEY }}

      - name: Add server to known hosts
        run: ssh-keyscan -H ${{ secrets.SERVER_IP }} >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts

      - name: Run deployment script on server
        run: |
          ssh deploy@${{ secrets.SERVER_IP }} 'bash /var/www/app/scripts/deploy.sh'
Setting Up GitHub Secrets
In your GitHub repo, go to Settings -> Secrets and Variables -> Actions and add:

items: DEPLOY_SSH_KEY - the private half of an SSH key pair. Add the public key to ~/.ssh/authorized_keys on your server for the deploy user.
SERVER_IP - your VPS IP address or hostname.

content.text: Nginx Config
Point Nginx at the current symlink, not a specific release:
Rolling Back
If a deploy goes wrong, rollback is a one-liner:

# Point current at the previous release
ln -nfs /var/www/app/releases/20250418110000 /var/www/app/current
php artisan queue:restart
Common Mistakes

items: Running migrations before the symlink swap with a breaking schema change - if your migration removes a column the old code needs, users on the old release will see errors during the swap window. Run additive migrations first, remove columns in a follow-up deploy.
Not setting set -e in the deploy script - without it, the script continues past failures and can flip a broken release into production.
Forgetting queue:restart - queue workers hold the old code in memory. Without a restart signal, they keep processing jobs with stale code after the deploy.
Leaving too many old releases - they eat disk space fast on a VPS. The cleanup step at the end of the script keeps only the last 3.

content.text: Final Takeaway

Zero-downtime deployment on a single VPS is not complex infrastructure - it's a 60-line bash script and a GitHub Actions workflow. Once it's in place, pushing to main is genuinely stress-free. The symlink swap is atomic. Rollback is instant. Old releases are your safety net.

Action step: set up the directory structure on your VPS today and do a test deploy manually before wiring up GitHub Actions. Understanding the script by running it yourself makes the automation much less scary.
